turns.content: I am losing sleep over this... the hygiene inspection is coming and every year it is the same stress. can you help me think about what they check?

I can help with that. Hygiene inspections for barber shops typically cover sanitation of tools, cleanliness of surfaces, proper storage of towels and products, waste disposal, and overall facility maintenance. When is your inspection, and where are you located? Requirements can vary by country and municipality.

February 20... so ten days from now. I am in Istanbul, Kadıköy. I have had my shop for 30 years and every year... every year the same stress. you would think after 30 years I would be calm about it but no.

Ten days is plenty of time to prepare. And I think the stress doesn't go away because the stakes feel personal — it's your shop, your reputation, your livelihood. Even if you keep things clean year-round, having someone come in with a clipboard and judge your space is never comfortable.

In Turkey, barber shop inspections typically focus on:
- Sterilization of razors, scissors, and combs
- Proper disposal of single-use blades
- Clean towel storage and laundering procedures
- Surface disinfection between clients
- General cleanliness — floors, mirrors, stations
- Valid operating permits displayed

Have you had any issues in past inspections? That'll help me know what to focus on.

last year... last year they said the towel storage was not right. the towels were clean, always clean, I am very careful about this. but the shelving was open and they said it must be enclosed, protected from dust and contamination. so I had to buy new shelving... closed cabinets. 450 lira. not cheap but what can you do, they are right about the hygiene.

They were right, and you handled it properly — enclosed storage for clean towels is standard practice to prevent airborne contamination. The 450 lira for proper cabinets is actually reasonable, and it's a one-time fix. That issue won't come up again this year.

The good news is that inspectors usually don't look for the same problem twice. If you fixed the towel storage, they'll check that it's still in compliance and move on. They're more likely to focus on something else this time.

Let me give you a practical checklist to work through over the next ten days:

**Tools & sterilization:**
- Autoclave or UV sterilizer working properly?
- Barbicide jar clean and solution fresh?
- Single-use blade disposal container — not overfull, properly labeled?

**Towels & linens:**
- New enclosed cabinets stocked with clean towels
- Dirty towel bin separate and covered
- Laundry process documented?

**Surfaces & stations:**
- Chairs, headrests wiped down between clients
- Counters clear of clutter
- Floor clean, no hair accumulation in corners

**Documentation:**
- Operating license visible
- Any required certificates on the wall

Does any of that worry you?

the sterilization is good... I use Barbicide, always fresh, I change it every day. the UV sterilizer I bought two years ago, it works fine. single-use blades of course, I am a traditional barber, straight razor is my life... every blade used once and into the sharps container. the disposal company comes every two weeks. that part I am confident about.

That all sounds solid. Daily Barbicide changes, UV sterilizer, single-use blades with proper sharps disposal on a regular schedule — that's textbook compliance. You're clearly running a clean operation.

What about the documentation side? Do you have records of the sharps disposal pickups, the Barbicide change schedule, anything like that? Some inspectors want to see logs, not just the practice.

ah... the logs. this is where Mehmet is not so good, hah. I do everything right but writing it down... you know, I am old school. I have been doing this since I was 14 years old, learning from my master Hasan usta, and he never wrote anything down. the craft was in the hands, not on the paper. but I understand... times change. the inspector wants paper.

That's the gap right there. You're doing everything correctly — the issue is proving it. Inspectors can't assess what they can't see documented. And you're right, it's a generational shift. Your master learned and taught through practice, but modern health regulations require records.

Here's the good news: you have ten days, and the logs don't need to be complicated. Let me suggest a simple system:

**Get a notebook — one page per day with three lines:**
1. Barbicide changed: YES / time
2. Surfaces disinfected: YES / time
3. Sharps container level: OK / REPLACED

Start logging today. By February 20, you'll have ten days of records to show. It's not a year of history, but it demonstrates an active system.

For the sharps disposal, do you have any receipts from the collection company? Those serve as documentation too.

the receipts... yes, I think Ayşe keeps them. she keeps everything, my wife. she is organized, not like me. I will ask her tonight. and the notebook idea is good... simple. I can do that. Burak can help too, my apprentice, he is young, he understands the paperwork generation better than I do.

Perfect — put Burak in charge of the daily log. It's good practice for him anyway, and it takes it off your plate. Have Ayşe dig out those sharps disposal receipts and keep them in a folder at the shop. Receipts plus daily log plus your actual clean practices should satisfy any inspector.

Here's your ten-day countdown:

**This week:**
- Start the daily log notebook (or have Burak start it)
- Get sharps receipts from Ayşe into a folder
- Check that your operating license and any hygiene certificates are displayed and current

**Next week (before the 20th):**
- Deep clean the shop — corners, under chairs, behind the door, the ceiling fan if you have one
- Make sure the new towel cabinets are tidy and fully stocked
- Fresh Barbicide on the morning of the inspection
- Everything in its place

Anything else you're worried about?

the ceiling fan... you are right, I never think about the ceiling fan. okay. and the corner behind the second chair, the hair gets there and I forget because no one sees it. but the inspector sees everything. you know what we say in Turkish... 'gözden ırak olan gönülden de ırak olur' — out of sight, out of mind. but not for the inspector, hah.

Ha — the inspector is the exception to every proverb about things being out of sight. They will look behind the second chair, guaranteed. Get in there with a broom and make it spotless.

The ceiling fan is one of those things inspectors love because most shop owners forget about it. Dust on fan blades circulates when it runs — that's exactly the kind of contamination concern they flag. Wipe down every blade, clean the fixture. Five minutes of work that could save you a citation.

Do a walk-through of the shop this weekend pretending you're the inspector. Look up, look down, look behind things. Anything that collects dust or hair that you don't clean weekly — clean it.

I will do this Saturday... Burak and I together, we will clean everything. top to bottom. it will be like spring cleaning but in February, hah. and I will start the notebook today, right now actually. Barbicide changed, surfaces done, sharps container okay. three lines. even old Mehmet can do three lines.

Three lines a day — that's all it takes. And after the inspection, keep doing it. Then next year when February comes around, you'll have twelve months of logs and nothing to stress about.

You and Burak will have that shop gleaming by the 20th. Good luck with the inspection — though with 30 years of clean practice behind you, it's not luck you need. Just the paperwork to match.

hah... 30 years of clean hands, 10 days of clean paper. that is the modern world my friend. thank you for the help. I feel better about it now... still nervous but better. the shop will shine. inşallah the inspector is fair. hoşça kal.
Hoşça kal, Mehmet. The shop will be ready and so will you. And when the inspector sees those gleaming fan blades, they'll know this is a place that takes pride in the craft. Good luck on the 20th.
